understand.plain: The function f(n) is a recursive function. For any n > 1, it first calls itself with n/2 (integer division), then prints n%2 (the remainder when divided by 2). The base case prints n when n <= 1. This pattern builds up the binary representation of n from most-significant bit to least-significant bit.

keywords.explain: Expressing a number using only digits 0 and 1, where each digit corresponds to a power of 2.
When printf comes after the recursive call, the deepest call's output appears first, building the result from most significant to least significant bit.
keywords.example: f(4): calls f(2) first, then prints 4%2=0; f(2) calls f(1) first then prints 0; f(1) prints 1 => output: 100

understand.visual_alt: Recursive call stack for f(173): each level calls f(n/2) first then prints n%2, so output is built from MSB to LSB giving 10101101 (binary of 173)

given.aim: Trace f(173) to find the printed output

terms.meaning: Shifts the number right by one bit in binary
terms.connects: Each recursive call strips off the least significant bit

terms.meaning: The remainder when n is divided by 2; gives the least significant bit of n
terms.connects: Printed after recursive call, so bits appear MSB first

terms.meaning: When n is 0 or 1, print it directly; this is the stopping condition
terms.connects: The first character printed in the output sequence

given.plan: Trace the recursion: repeatedly divide n by 2 until reaching the base case, then unwind the stack printing n%2 at each level. The output reads as the binary representation of n from MSB to LSB.

steps.title: Convert 173 to binary
steps.apply:
173 / 2 = 86 r 1
86 / 2 = 43 r 0
43 / 2 = 21 r 1
21 / 2 = 10 r 1
10 / 2 = 5 r 0
5 / 2 = 2 r 1
2 / 2 = 1 r 0
1 <= 1: base case, print 1
Reading remainders bottom to top: 1 0 1 0 1 1 0 1
steps.note: The recursive structure ensures MSB is printed first because each level prints after its recursive call returns

steps.title: Reconstruct the printed output
steps.apply: Stack unwinds: f(1) prints 1, then f(2) prints 0, then f(5) prints 1, then f(10) prints 0, then f(21) prints 1, then f(43) prints 1, then f(86) prints 0, then f(173) prints 1. Final output: 10101101

questions.formula_note: The function implements binary-number-to-string conversion using recursion; print-after-recursive-call pattern ensures MSB is output first.

questions.to_find: Which implementations P1 and/or P2 produce the same output as f(173)

understand.plain: The original function f prints the binary representation of n. We need to check if P1 and P2 also print the binary representation of 173 (which is 10101101). P1 is a simplified recursive version, and P2 uses bit masking to extract each bit from MSB to LSB.

keywords.explain: Using bitwise AND (&) with a power-of-2 mask to test whether a specific bit of a number is set (1) or not (0).
Finding the highest power of 2 <= n, then repeatedly checking and halving the mask to print each bit from MSB to LSB.
keywords.example: For n=6 (110): mask starts at 4, prints 1; mask=2, prints 1; mask=1, prints 0 => '110'

understand.visual_alt: P1 uses recursion with print-after-call just like original but prints n%2 even for base case (giving same result since 1%2=1 and 0%2=0). P2 uses iterative bit masking from MSB to LSB.

given.aim: Determine which of P1 and P2 produce the same binary output as f(173) = 10101101

terms.meaning: Recursive: if n > 1, call f1(n/2), then always print n%2
terms.example: f1(2): calls f1(1), then prints 2%2=0; f1(1): prints 1%2=1 => '10' same as f(2)
terms.connects: The base case prints n%2 which equals n for n=0 or 1, so output is identical to original f

terms.meaning: Finds highest power of 2 <= n, then extracts each bit using bitwise AND, from MSB to LSB
terms.connects: Produces the binary representation of n from MSB to LSB, same as f
Both P1 and P2 must match this exact output

given.plan: Verify P1: trace through the recursion for n=173 and check if it prints the same sequence. Verify P2: check if the mask-based loop correctly extracts bits from MSB to LSB for n=173.

steps.title: Verify P1 produces same output as f
steps.formula_raw: f1(n): if n>1 then f1(n/2); print n%2
steps.apply: P1 differs from original only in base case: original prints 'n' when n<=1, P1 prints 'n%2'. For n=1: 1%2=1 (same). For n=0: 0%2=0 (same). In the recursive path, both call f(n/2) first then print n%2. So P1 is functionally identical to original f for all positive integers. P1 produces 10101101 for n=173.
steps.note: The simplification in P1 works because for the base case n<=1, n%2 == n always holds

steps.title: Verify P2 produces same output as f
steps.formula_raw: n & mask: extracts bit at position corresponding to mask
steps.apply:
For n=173: highest power of 2 <= 173 is 128. mask sequence: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1.
173 & 128 = 128 (nonzero) -> print 1
173 & 64 = 0 -> print 0
173 & 32 = 32 (nonzero) -> print 1
173 & 16 = 0 -> print 0
173 & 8 = 8 (nonzero) -> print 1
173 & 4 = 4 (nonzero) -> print 1
173 & 2 = 0 -> print 0
173 & 1 = 1 (nonzero) -> print 1
Output: 10101101. Matches f(173).
steps.note: P2 uses an iterative approach with bit masking; it correctly produces binary output from MSB to LSB

solution.result: Both P1 and P2 produce the same output as f(173). Answer: A. Both P1 and P2

questions.formula_note: Bitwise AND for bit extraction; recursive binary printing pattern; both approaches correctly compute binary representation from MSB to LSB.

questions.question:
The usual O(n^2) implementation of Insertion Sort to sort an array uses linear search to identify the position where an element is to be inserted into the already-sorted part of the array. If, instead, we use binary search to identify the position, the worst case running time will

A. remain Theta(n^2)
B. become Theta(n (log n)^2)
C. become Theta(n log n)
D. become Theta(n)
questions.to_find: Worst-case running time of Insertion Sort when binary search is used to find the insertion position

understand.plain: Insertion Sort works by taking each element and inserting it into its correct position in the already-sorted portion. Normally, finding the right position uses linear search (O(n) comparisons). If we switch to binary search, we can find the position in O(log n) comparisons. However, we still need to physically shift elements to make room, and that shifting still takes O(n) time in the worst case. So the total work per element is O(n), and overall it remains O(n^2).

keywords.explain: Instead of scanning left to right to find where an element belongs, use binary search to find the position in O(log n) comparisons.
keywords.example: If sorted part is [1,3,5,7,9] and we insert 4, binary search finds position between 3 and 5 in O(log 5) = 3 comparisons instead of 4

keywords.explain: After finding the insertion position, all elements to the right of that position must be shifted one place to the right to make room.
keywords.example: Inserting 4 into [1,3,5,7,9]: 5,7,9 all shift right -> [1,3,4,5,7,9]. This takes O(n) time.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='260' rx='10' fill='#F8F7F2'/><text x='260' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Insertion Sort Cost Analysis</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='500' height='100' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='62' class='tag ink'>Per-element cost breakdown (inserting i-th element into sorted part of size i-1):</text><rect x='20' y='72' width='220' height='36' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Find position:</text><text x='130' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>O(log i) with binary search</text><rect x='260' y='72' width='240' height='36' rx='4' class='no'/><text x='380' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Shift elements:</text><text x='380' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>O(i) always (dominates!)</text><rect x='10' y='150' width='500' height='90' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='172' class='tag ink'>Total cost = sum_{i=1}^{n} [O(log i) + O(i)]</text><text x='20' y='198' class='sub mut'>= O(sum of log i) + O(sum of i)</text><text x='20' y='218' class='sub mut'>= O(n log n)   +   O(n^2)</text><text x='20' y='242' class='lbl ink'>= Theta(n^2)  (shifting dominates)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Cost breakdown per element insertion: finding position with binary search costs O(log i), but shifting elements costs O(i). Total = O(n log n) + O(n^2) = O(n^2). The shift cost dominates.

given.aim: Find worst-case time complexity of Insertion Sort using binary search for position finding

terms.meaning: Number of comparisons needed to find where to insert element i into sorted array of size i-1
terms.example: Linear search: O(i); Binary search: O(log i)
terms.connects: Binary search reduces comparison count but does NOT reduce shifting cost

terms.meaning: Number of element moves needed after finding the insertion position; equals the number of elements to the right of insertion point
terms.example: Inserting at position 0 in sorted array of size i-1 requires i-1 shifts
terms.connects: In worst case (reverse-sorted input), every insertion requires O(i) shifts; this is the bottleneck

terms.meaning: Sum over all n insertions of the per-element cost
terms.connects: Even with O(log i) comparisons per step, the O(i) shift cost per step means overall cost stays Theta(n^2)

given.plan: Analyze the two components of each insertion: (1) finding the correct position (O(log i) with binary search vs O(i) with linear), and (2) shifting elements right to make room (O(i) in worst case regardless of search method). The shifting cost dominates and determines the overall complexity.

steps.title: Analyze cost per insertion step
steps.apply:
For inserting the i-th element into a sorted array of size i-1:
- With binary search: comparison cost = O(log i)
- Shift cost = O(i) in worst case (when element belongs at position 0, all i-1 elements shift right)
So Cost_i = O(log i) + O(i) = O(i)
steps.note: Binary search only saves comparisons, not data movement. The shift operation is inherent to array-based insertion sort.

steps.title: Sum over all n elements
steps.apply:
Total comparisons with binary search: sum_{i=1}^{n} O(log i) = O(n log n)
Total shifts: sum_{i=1}^{n} O(i) = O(1+2+3+...+n) = O(n(n+1)/2) = O(n^2)
Overall: T(n) = O(n log n) + O(n^2) = Theta(n^2)
steps.note: Using binary search reduces comparisons from Theta(n^2) to Theta(n log n), but the shift cost of Theta(n^2) is unchanged. Therefore worst case remains Theta(n^2).

solution.result: Worst-case running time remains Theta(n^2). Answer: A. remain Theta(n^2)

questions.formula_note: Insertion Sort time complexity: O(n^2) due to element shifting, which dominates even when binary search reduces comparison cost to O(n log n). Key insight: finding position and moving elements are separate costs.
